title: Mayan Forest Biodiversity Conservation Program Workbook
text:
Overview
The workbook documents the SD VISta Nature Framework Methodology for quantification of nature credits. This includes quantifying nature benefits and setting a crediting baseline for validation.
This site is structured to support validation, verification, and audit transparency.

title: Mayan Forest Biodiversity Conservation Program Workbook

- Ecosystem: Tropical/Sub-tropical Dry Forest

title: Project Extent
text:
The ecosystem typology for the Project Extent was determined differently than the instructions of the Nature Framework. This is a project deviation that was needed to accurately define the ecosystem typology in the Project Extent. When using the IUCN GET level 3 classifications and tool for the Project Extent (part of the Yucatan Peninsula, Mexico), the data was inaccurate and deficient. For instance, the main function groups that were found in the Project Extent included intensive landscapes like annual croplands, plantations, and urban and industrial ecosystems.


Link CSV File Here


However, these functional groups are not true for the Project Extent, as Terra Global has already worked in this area since 2022 and conducted ground truthing. These areas are primarily forested and many of the ejidos included in the program are in the buffer zone of the Calakmul Biosphere Reserve, a largely intact Mayan forest remnant home to much of the wildlife and one of the largest populations of jaguars left in the Yucatan peninsula.
Thus, the data used to define the ecosystem types of the Project Extent was based on research and literature (Jung, et al., 2020). The data was based on the IUCN Global Database that was later created and used for the methodology, so it is still appropriate to use in this case. This data also allowed the program to refine the Project Extent to the 9 ejidos that are included in the Program and it provided much more accurate results.


Link SHP File Here


The main functional group that was found in all of the ejidos was the Tropical/Sub-tropical Dry forests and thickets. While there were several ecosystem types found in each ejido (Map 1), these ecosystem types were excluded due to being arable land, plantations, or urban areas that are not eligible under the methodology. Furthermore, other ecosystem types, like wetlands and sub-tropical/tropical swamps only made up 1.44% and 0.78% respectively, in the largest areas of the ejidos. The CSV file for the different ecosystem types per ejido can also be found here.


Link CSV File Here


This file also contains how the other functional groups were excluded and the total number of dry forest per ejido was summed (find explanation in Project Extent Calculations).

Project Spatial Boundary





Map 1. IUCN Functional Groups (Ecosystem Types) found in the Project Boundary



Project Extent Calculations
While dry savanna make up part of the ecosystem types for some ejidos, it was ultimately excluded from the Project Extent, as program activities are centered to be implemented in the dry forest ecosystems, and any ecosystem that makes up less than 35% of an ejido is excluded due to their limited spatial extent, which reduces their influence on landscape-scale ecological processes and biodiversity outcomes. Prioritizing ecosystem types that represent a more substantial portion of the Project Extent allows for more efficient allocation of resources, more robust monitoring, and greater potential for measurable impact. However, dry savannas are native ecosystems in the project extent and future instances will include this ecosystem.
Following the exclusion of the hectares in the other functional groups, the Table 1 and Map 1 were created to show the number of hectares of Tropical/sub-tropical dry forests in each ejido and the total hectares for the Project Extent.

title: Step 2. Condition indicators
text:
Composition Indicators
The five indicators that were chosen were based on the data available from the first baseline assessment of the area done for the corresponding VCS/CCB Project (Project ID 3479 IFM V0010). These are also important indicators for overall ecosystem health and function. The composition indicators that are used include mammal species richness, avian species richness, and herpetofauna (amphibians and reptiles) species richness. The structure indicators that are used for the quantification include aboveground biomass and canopy cover, both suitable indicators to inform on ecosystem health and function.

title: Step 2. Condition indicators
text:
Mammal Species Richness
Mammal species richness is an appropriate indicator of biodiversity in sub-tropical dry forests as it is sensitive to habitat structure, resource availability, and anthropogenic pressures. Mammals occupy diverse ecological niches, and their presence often reflects overall ecosystem integrity, which serves as an effective proxy for measuring impacts of program activities on the environment (Ceballos & Ehlrich, 2002). Additionally, mammals are relatively easy to monitor through camera traps and transect surveys, which are easily replicable for monitoring over time, even in remote areas. Species richness is a simple analysis that can be done by basic training if communities are to take over on biodiversity monitoring.
Mammals were primarily monitored using camera traps that were placed in each ejido. The following pre-approved Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) was used in the nine ejidos. 10 Browing® Dark Ops APEX 20MP brand camera traps or similar equipment was placed, except for those ejidos in which there were logistics problems in the field and only 5 or 6 cameras were placed. Each camera trap was placed in a site with natural vegetation coverage and preferably at the intersection of 2 to 3 trails, they were attached to the base of the trunks at a height of 40 cm from the ground. The cameras were set to take a photo and a 15-second video, they remained active 24 hours a day for the duration of the study (2 weeks).


Avian Species Richness
Bird species richness is an appropriate indicator for sub-tropical dry forests due to birds’ ecological sensitivity, habitat specificity, and functional diversity. The dry forests in the Project Extent are characterized by seasonal rainfall, deciduous vegetation, and high biodiversity, which allows for a wide array of bird species. Many of these species are habitat specialists or migratory species that rely on highly intact structure and seasonal resources. Birds respond quickly to changes in vegetation cover, forest structure and water availability, making them reliable early indicators of ecosystem health. Program activities are aimed at improving forest structure through corridor development, conservation efforts, and increasing water availability in response to climate change. Changes in species richness over the program lifetime could be reliably attributed to changes due to program activities.
Avian species were monitored with point counts along transects during the two week long baseline biodiversity assessment in 2023. The following procedure was used to collect observations of avian species. The counting method was used by transects separated by 1 km between each transect. The criterion for choosing the transects was the proximity of the points where the camera traps were installed but separated at the beginning between 200 and 300 meters, so that the noise from the installation of the cameras would not affect the bird recording. The observation points were separated from each other every 100 m, at each point all the birds observed were recorded for 10 minutes.


Herpetofauna Species Richness
Amphibians and reptiles are highly sensitive to changes in the environment. Reptiles (e.g., lizards, snakes, and turtles) are adapted to the warm, seasonally dry conditions characteristic of these forests. Many species are habitat specialists, occupying niches tied to particular vegetation types, ground cover, or thermal environments. Their population dynamics and community composition can change in response to alterations in land use, canopy cover, or fire regimes, making them effective indicators of habitat quality and connectivity.
Amphibians, while generally associated with moist habitats, are also important in dry forests — especially those that rely on ephemeral water sources for reproduction. Their permeable skin makes them vulnerable to changes in temperature, humidity, water availability, and pollutants. As a result, amphibians provide early warning signs of ecosystem degradation, particularly regarding hydrological balance and microhabitat integrity.
Monitoring the richness of both groups captures complementary aspects of ecosystem health: reptiles reflect structural and thermal habitat features, while amphibians respond to moisture dynamics and microhabitat quality. Additionally, these taxa are often underrepresented in conservation assessments, so their inclusion supports a more holistic and taxonomically inclusive biodiversity strategy. It is important to note that monitoring did not occur during the wet season, when herpetofauna richness and diversity would be at its highest. Unfortunately, due to the remoteness of most of the ejidos and the transects, it is nearly impossible to navigate to some of them in the wet season. While we would not expect to see the highest species richness each monitoring period, we do expect to see moderate richness and will provide a good indication of ecosystem health even in the dry season, when climate may be most impactful on the environment.
The following procedure was used to monitor the herpetofauna species in the Project Extent. Transects of 1000 meters in length and 5 meters in width was chosen, during which a thorough search was carried out in all the microhabitats present within the transect, where the presence of herpetofauna was considered probable (under stones, under logs, in tree cavities, between tree bark, in burrows in the ground, etc.).
The number of transects in each ejido varied depending on its area; we sought to cover the greatest heterogeneity of the landscape present in each locality. The lizards were captured using thick rubber bands, throwing them by stretching them with a finger and oriented towards the individual, so that when hitting it they produce a temporary immobilization that allows their capture without causing damage to the specimen. The study was complemented by reviewing water sources and wells in those locations where there were bodies of water. The amphibians and reptiles found were identified to species level using specialized field guides and were recorded in a field log, along with the number of the specimen, the ejido where it was found, the number of the transect and any characteristic or observation of importance for this monitoring.

title: Step 2. Condition indicators
text:
Structure Indicators

Aboveground biomass
Aboveground biomass is a widely used indicator for ecosystem structure, carbon storage, and habitat quality in subtropical dry forests. Aboveground biomass reflects the accumulation of woody materials (trees, shrubs, and lianas), which influences the ecological functions and services of the ecosystem. In dry forest ecosystems, changes in AGB are related to land-use practices such as deforestation, grazing, and fuelwood extraction. Thus, monitoring AGB helps detect degradation and recovery over time, providing insight into the effectiveness of program activities (i.e., Assisted Native Regeneration in wildlife corridors, improved forest management). AGB is quantifiable through standardized field measurements and remote sensing, making it a practical and scalable indicator for monitoring ecosystem condition across large areas and over time.


Canopy Cover
Canopy cover is a suitable indicator for subtropical dry forests because it reflects forest condition, habitat quality, and restoration progress. It influences microclimate, soil moisture, and biodiversity, particularly for forest-dependent species—and can be easily monitored using remote sensing or field tools. Its sensitivity to disturbance and recovery makes it a reliable measure of ecosystem health.

title: Step 3. Define reference values for Condition indicators
text:
Evidence of the documented inventory used to establish reference values for Condition indicators is maintained through a structured compilation of peer-reviewed literature, spatial datasets, and reproducible analytical workflows. All data sources, selection criteria, and analytical steps were recorded and archived to ensure transparency, traceability, and auditability in accordance with Verra requirements.
For biodiversity composition indicators, including mammal, bird, and herpetofauna species richness, the documented inventory consists of peer-reviewed scientific studies published from 2000 onward and primarily conducted in the Calakmul Biosphere Reserve (CBR) and the broader Yucatan Peninsula. The CBR was selected as the primary reference area because it represents near-pristine ecological conditions where most anthropogenic activities are prohibited. Literature searches were conducted systematically using Google Scholar with predefined keyword combinations such as “species richness,” “diversity,” “Calakmul Biosphere Reserve,” and “Yucatan Peninsula,” tailored to each taxonomic group. Records of search terms, publication dates, and inclusion criteria were maintained as part of the inventory documentation.
For mammal species richness, the inventory includes camera-trap and field-based studies focusing on different mammalian groups within the CBR. These include studies on ungulates documenting six species (Reyna-Hurtado & Tanner, 2007) and broader assessments of large terrestrial mammals across the Yucatan Peninsula, identifying a total of 14 large mammal species (Reyna-Hurtado et al., 2015). Because many studies focused on specific taxa, results were synthesized across multiple sources to produce a comprehensive mammal species inventory (excluding bats), resulting in a reference value of 46 species. All source publications are cited and archived as part of the documented inventory.
For bird species richness, the inventory draws on multiple avifaunal surveys conducted within the CBR. These include a study assessing bird abundance across feeding guilds that identified more than 300 species (Walsh, 2018), as well as a comprehensive inventory documenting 403 bird species, which represents the most extensive avian dataset available for the reserve (González et al., 2016). These studies were used to inform a conservative reference value of 230 bird species, reflecting expected richness under intact ecological conditions while accounting for methodological variability among surveys.
For herpetofauna species richness, the documented inventory includes post-2000 studies conducted in the CBR using standardized field methods such as Visual Encounter Surveys and pitfall traps. A key study documented the presence of 20 amphibian species and 69 reptile species, resulting in a total of 90 herpetofauna species under near-pristine conditions (Colston et al., 2015). This study and supporting literature were archived as evidence of the reference condition.
For structural condition indicators where historical field-based reference data were limited, the documented inventory consists of spatially explicit remote sensing datasets and derived statistical analyses. Canopy cover reference values were based on Terra MODIS Vegetation Continuous Fields (VCF) products at 250 m resolution, generated annually using monthly composites of Terra MODIS land surface reflectance and temperature data across all seven spectral bands. Datasets covering the ten years preceding project start (t–10 to t=0) were included in the inventory, along with ecosystem classification layers and Habitat Conversion Risk maps used to filter pixels.
Aboveground biomass reference values were derived from a documented inventory of GEDI Level 4 Aboveground Biomass data combined with Sentinel-1 synthetic aperture radar and Sentinel-2 multispectral imagery. A Random Forest regression model was trained annually using approximately 4,000 GEDI plots per year to predict AGB across the program’s ecoregion. All input datasets, model parameters, training samples, and output rasters were stored with accompanying metadata describing spatial resolution, temporal coverage, and ecosystem filtering criteria.
All analytical procedures used to derive percentile-based reference values (75th percentile for canopy cover and 90th percentile for aboveground biomass) were fully documented, including pixel selection rules, statistical assumptions, and sample sizes. Reproducible scripts and summary outputs were archived to enable independent verification.

title: Step 4. Measure Condition indicators at project start
text:
Mammal Species Richness
While the project does have historical start date, biodiversity monitoring was conducted two weeks after the start date, thus, we did not need to use proxy sites.
The following Table 4 includes the condition at project start for each indicator, and each subsection provides an explanation on how these were calculated.
Each camera trap had a unique name and GPS coordinate to identify which camera captured which animal. Once we were able to get the raw data, we also needed to ensure that the data collected from the baseline assessment of the carbon project aligned with the Nature Framework requirements. We found that the data collected at each camera trap could have been associated with a different functional group, so we took each location of camera trap and added it to the shapefile of the functional groups. We then cross referenced this file to the raw data collected in the field according to the camera trap ID and coordinates. If the camera trap was in an area not described as Tropical/Sub-tropical dry forests, the data was excluded.
To calculate the species richness, we measured the total number of species observed in each ejido and added them together for the Project Extent, while excluding any species that was found outside of the tropical/sub-tropical dry forests.

title: Step 4. Measure Condition indicators at project start
text:
Avian Species Richness
At each point, the geographical coordinates, point number, time, species observed/heard, number of individuals per species, distance and observation height were taken. Another aspect to mention, in some points more transects were made and in others less, due to the conditions of the terrain and surface of the area. The recorded species were identified using binoculars (8x42) and the field guide “Go out and bird Yucatán” (2014) and the Merlin application (The Cornell Lab).
Each coordinate for point counts (observation points) was included in the raw data and were cross referenced with the location of each coordinate and the associated functional group. Any point and corresponding data was excluded if it was not located in the Tropical/Sub-tropical dry forests.
To calculate the species richness, we measured the total number of species observed in each ejido and added them together for the Project Extent, while excluding any species that was found outside of the tropical/sub-tropical dry forests.

title: Step 6. Calculate Condition at project start (year 0)
text:
The following arithmetic mean equation was followed to calculate the condition of the indicators at project start. \[
C_0 = \frac{\frac{St_1 + St_2 + \cdots + St_n}{n} + \frac{Cm_1 + Cm_2 + \cdots + Cm_n}{n}}{2}
\]
Where:

\(C_0\) = Condition at project start (Year 0)

\(St\) = Standardized structure indicator at project start (year 0) (calculated as SI0 in Step 5)

\(Cm\) = Standardized composition indicator at project start (year 0) (calculated as SI0 in Step 5)

\(n\) = Total number of structure and composition indicators

The calculation can be found below:
\[
\frac{\left(\frac{0.812 + 0.833}{2}\right) + \left(\frac{0.511 + 0.935 + 0.674}{3}\right)}{2}=0.76
\]

title: Step 8. Crediting Baseline
text:
Step H1. Map buffer zone around project Extent
The spatial boundaries of the project extent were digitally mapped using QGIS and provided as a .geojson file. Per the SD Vista Nature Framework v1.0 Habitat conversion Risk Method, budder zones around the project extent are not required for baseline calculation. The methodology requires baseline calculations only within the project extent where conservation activities are implemented and credits will be claimed.

title: Step 8. Crediting Baseline
section: Step H2. Choose structure indicator for modeling
text:
Step H2. Choose structure indicator for modeling
The structural indicator used for the Habitat Conversion Risk method is one of the structure indicators chosen for monitoring, that is Canopy Cover. As such, it is a target indicator, and not a proxy indicator, and as such it did not require quantifying the correlation with its proxy (i.e. the indicator is not a proxy). For a justification as to why this index was used, refer to Table 3.


Table 8. Condition indicator employed for quantify habitat loss risk










Condition Indicator
Target or Proxy
Credible Source & Justification




Canopy Cover
Structural Indicator – Target
Canopy Cover directly measures habitat structure and is highly sensitive to both ecosystem degradation and conversion. The indicator has 10 years of continuous historical data, enabling regression-based forecasting as required by SD VISta for using historical conversion rates. MODIS is a credible, globally recognized source with documented accuracy and validation. Spatial resolution (250m) complies with SD VISta requirement of ≤300m. The indicator is already used for program monitoring, ensuring consistency between baseline and verification. Compliance with Section 7.5.4: Dataset meets all data requirements including spatial resolution, temporal coverage, credible source, and sensitivity to habitat conversion.

title: Step 8. Crediting Baseline
section: Step H3. Create or source a raster of the structure indicator
text:
Step H3. Create or source a raster of the structure indicator
A probability raster was generated using regression analysis fitted to 10 years of historical Canopy Cover data. This approach fulfills SD VISta’s requirements to use historical conversion rate for predicting future habitat conditions. This approach employs pixel-level linear regression models that incorporate both spatial and temporal drivers of habitat conversion, allowing for spatially explicit forecasts that reflect observed historical trends.


Table 9. Sources and Justification for Covariates Used for Crediting Baseline









Covariate
Credible Source & Justification




IUCN Habitat Map
Jung et al. (2020) was utilized to characterize and describe the Project Extent, applying the IUCN habitat classification and counting pixels per habitat class.


MODIS NPP (Response Variable)
The response variable for the regression analysis is the MODIS Tree Cover dataset (MOD44B Collection 6.1), which provides annual measurements of tree canopy cover. This 10-year time series serves as the dependent variable in the regression models, with each pixel containing 10 annual observations. The dataset has a spatial resolution of 250m × 250m, complying with SD VISta’s requirement of ≤300m resolution. MODIS Tree Cover is a credible, globally recognized data source with documented accuracy and validation procedures. The 10-year temporal coverage provides sufficient historical data to detect meaningful trends and fit robust regression models. Geographic coverage includes both the reference region and project extent, ensuring that baseline calculations are conducted across all relevant areas. Data were obtained from NASA Earth Observations and processed as a multi-band GeoTIFF file, with each band representing one year of observations.


CISI (Static Spatial Covariate)
To account for spatial variation in conversion risk, the regression models incorporate the Critical Infrastructure & Societal Index (CISI) as a static covariate. CISI is derived from the Harmonized Global Critical Infrastructure & Societal Index developed by Nirandjan et al. (2022), which provides a spatially explicit measure of human pressure based on proximity to critical anthropogenic infrastructure including roads, settlements, and urban areas. Distance from infrastructure is a well-established driver of deforestation and forest degradation in tropical forest landscapes. Areas closer to roads and settlements typically experience higher conversion pressure due to greater accessibility for agricultural expansion, logging, and other land-use activities. The CISI dataset provides a continuous measure of this spatial gradient of human influence.


Year (Temporal Covariate)
This predictor variable represents time in the regression models, included as a simple linear year variable. The temporal covariate captures the historical trend in tree cover change over the 10-year observation period. The coefficient associated with the year variable represents the average annual rate of change (increase or decline) in tree cover at each pixel, after accounting for the influence of infrastructure proximity.






Modeling approach:
The probability raster was generated using a pixel-level regression approach. For each pixel in the study area, a separate linear regression model was fitted to the 10-year historical time series of tree cover. This spatially explicit approach allows conversion risks to vary across the landscape based on local conditions and trends, rather than imposing a uniform average across all areas.
The regression model for each pixel takes the following form: TreeCover at time t equals β₀ plus β₁ times CISI plus β₂ times Year plus ε, where TreeCover is the observed tree cover percentage at time t serving as the response variable, β₀ is the intercept term estimated from the data, β₁ is the coefficient for CISI representing the relationship between infrastructure proximity and tree cover, CISI is the Critical Infrastructure and Societal Index value at the pixel which remains constant over time, β₂ is the coefficient for Year representing the historical conversion rate measured in percentage points of tree cover change per year, Year is the temporal variable , ε is the error term assumed to be normally distributed with mean zero and variance σ², and σ is the standard error of the regression which quantifies prediction uncertainty.
The model is fitted using ordinary least squares (OLS) regression, which estimates the coefficients β₀, β₁, and β₂ that minimize the sum of squared residuals between observed and predicted values. For each pixel, the regression uses 10 data points (one per year) to estimate three parameters, providing seven degrees of freedom for statistical inference, which is adequate for robust parameter estimation.
The coefficient β₁ quantifies the spatial relationship between infrastructure proximity and tree cover. A negative β₁ indicates that pixels closer to infrastructure (higher CISI values) tend to have lower tree cover, consistent with accessibility-driven deforestation patterns. The magnitude of β₁ indicates how strongly infrastructure proximity affects tree cover at that location. The coefficient β₂ quantifies the temporal trend in tree cover over the historical period. A negative β₂ indicates declining tree cover (deforestation), while a positive β₂ would indicate increasing tree cover (reforestation or regeneration). The magnitude of β₂ represents the historical conversion rate in percentage points per year, which is the key parameter for projecting future conditions.
Once the regression model is fitted to historical data, it is used to generate forecasts for the end of the monitoring period, defined as current year plus 5 years. For each pixel, the forecast is calculated by extrapolating the fitted relationship forward in time using the equation: Forecast at t₀ plus 5 equals β₀ plus β₁ times CISI plus β₂ times (Current_Year plus 5). This forecast represents the expected tree cover value if historical trends continue without intervention. Because CISI is static, its contribution to the forecast remains constant. The temporal component, β₂ times Year, drives the predicted change, with the 5-year projection multiplying the annual conversion rate by the length of the monitoring period.


Model Outputs:
The regression analysis generates two distinct outputs, both required for the SD VISta Habitat Conversion Risk Method. These outputs serve different purposes in the baseline calculation methodology and must not be conflated.
The first output is a raster of forecast values, representing the predicted tree cover at each pixel at the end of the monitoring period, defined as t₀ plus 5 years. These values are calculated directly from the regression equation by substituting the future time value. The forecast values provide the magnitude of expected change based on historical conversion rates embedded in the β₂ coefficient. They are expressed in the same units as the original indicator, namely percentage tree cover on a 0 to 100 scale. The forecast values are used in Step H7 of the methodology to project estimated ecosystem condition to the end of the monitoring period. By comparing forecast values to baseline (current) values, the methodology quantifies the absolute change in tree cover expected under the business-as-usual scenario. This magnitude-based approach ensures that the baseline reflects actual predicted changes in ecosystem condition, measured in meaningful biophysical units rather than abstract probability values.
The second output is a probability raster, representing the likelihood that future tree cover will decline below current levels at each pixel. This probability is calculated using the forecast uncertainty (standard error) from the regression model according to the formula: P(decline) equals Φ times (I at t₀ minus Forecast divided by σ), where P(decline) is the probability that future tree cover will be below current tree cover, Φ is the cumulative distribution function (CDF) of the standard normal distribution, I at t₀ is the current tree cover value at baseline, Forecast is the predicted future value from the regression, and σ is the standard error of the regression representing prediction uncertainty.
This formula calculates how many standard errors below the forecast the current value lies, then converts this standardized distance into a probability using the normal cumulative distribution function. If the forecast predicts substantial decline, meaning the forecast is much less than current value, the probability will be high, approaching 1. If the forecast predicts stable or increasing conditions, meaning the forecast is greater than or equal to current value, the probability will be low, approaching. The probability values range from 0 to 1, with values close to 0 indicating low likelihood of decline and values close to 1 indicating high likelihood of decline.
These probabilities are used in Steps H4 through H6 to classify pixels as high-risk when probability is greater than or equal to 0.3, or low risk when probability is less than 0.3, and to calculate the weighted proportion of the project extent facing conversion risk. The probability raster serves as a risk classification layer, identifying where habitat conversion is most likely to occur within the project extent, without quantifying the magnitude of that conversion.


Statistical Justification:
The probability calculation relies on the statistical assumption that forecast errors are normally distributed around the predicted value with mean zero. This assumption is standard in regression-based forecasting and is justified for ecosystem change models where errors arise from natural variability, measurement uncertainty, and stochastic processes that cannot be perfectly predicted. By using the standard normal cumulative distribution function, the methodology converts the forecast error into a probability statement. This probabilistic framework appropriately reflects the inherent uncertainty in predicting future ecosystem conditions. It acknowledges that forecasts are not deterministic but rather represent expected values with associated uncertainty bands. Areas where the forecast strongly predicts decline, meaning the forecast is many standard errors below the current value, receive high probabilities approaching 1. Areas with uncertain or stable forecasts, where the forecast is within one or two standard errors of the current value, receive moderate probabilities. Areas with increasing forecasts receive low probabilities approaching 0.
This approach is scientifically sound and provides a transparent, replicable method for quantifying conversion risk. The probability values are empirically derived from the data itself through the standard error rather than being arbitrarily assigned, ensuring that risk classifications reflect actual historical variability and predictability at each location. Pixels where tree cover has been highly variable historically will have larger standard errors and more moderate probabilities, while pixels with consistent declining trends will have smaller standard errors and more extreme (high or low) probabilities.


Outputs provided:
Two GeoTIFF files were generated and are provided as deliverables for this step.
The first file, forecast_values.tif, contains the regression-based forecast values for all pixels in the study area. The technical specifications are as follows: compressed float32 GeoTIFF format for efficient storage; projection matching the project extent shapefile, specifically WGS84 UTM Zone 16N; no-data value set to -1 for pixels outside the analysis area or with insufficient data; spatial resolution of 250m by 250m which complies with SD VISta’s requirement of no more than 300m; geographic coverage encompassing the reference region and project extent to enable calculations across all required areas; units expressed as percentage tree cover on a 0 to 100 scale; and a single band representing the forecast for t₀ plus 5 years. This raster provides spatially explicit predictions of future tree cover across the landscape, enabling visual identification of areas expected to experience the greatest declines. High-risk hotspots can be identified as areas with low forecast values indicating substantial predicted decline, which can inform program design and prioritization of conservation interventions.
The second file, probability_of_decay.tif, contains the probability of decline values for all pixels in the study area. The technical specifications mirror those of the forecast file: compressed float32 GeoTIFF format; projection matching the project extent shapefile; no-data value of -1; spatial resolution of 250m by 250m; coverage of reference region and project extent. However, the units differ fundamentally. Probability values are expressed on a 0 to 1 scale, where 0 represents 0% probability of decline and 1 represents 100% probability of decline. This raster serves as the primary input to Step H4, where the 0.3 probability threshold is applied to classify high-risk pixels. It provides a risk classification surface that quantifies the spatial distribution of conversion threat across the project landscape. When overlaid with the project extent boundary, this raster enables calculation of the weighted proportion of high-risk area, which directly informs the slope of the crediting baseline according to SD VISta principles.


Compliance statement:
This approach fulfills SD VISta’s requirement to model “spatially explicit probabilities of habitat conversion using historical conversion rates” as stated in Section 4.2.1 of the methodology. The regression-based forecasting methodology satisfies all key requirements of the framework through multiple mechanisms.
First, the approach uses historical conversion rates by fitting regression models to 10 years of observed data. The temporal coefficient β₂ in each pixel’s regression model captures the actual conversion trend over this period measured in percentage points per year, and projecting this coefficient forward embeds historical rates into future predictions rather than relying on expert judgment or literature values. Second, the methodology provides spatially explicit outputs where each individual pixel receives its own regression model, forecast value, and probability value based on local conditions and trends. This spatial heterogeneity allows conversion risk to vary across the landscape, reflecting the reality that some areas face higher pressure than others due to differences in accessibility, land tenure, and historical management. Third, the approach generates probability surfaces as required by SD VISta. The probability_of_decay.tif raster quantifies the likelihood of decline at each location, providing both the magnitude through forecast values and the likelihood through probability values of habitat conversion. These dual outputs enable the risk-based weighting approach specified in the methodology.
Fourth, the methodology incorporates predictors of habitat loss, specifically the CISI covariate representing a well-established driver of deforestation, namely infrastructure proximity. This ensures that forecasts reflect not only temporal trends, but also spatial patterns of conversion risk driven by accessibility and human pressure gradients.
Fifth, the outputs enable the risk-based baseline calculations required in Steps H4 through H11. The probability raster supports risk classification in H4, exclusion zone application in H5, and risk weighting in H6. The forecast raster supports magnitude calculations in H7 through H11. Together, these outputs provide all information needed to calculate a conservative, defensible crediting baseline that approriately reflects both the likelihood and magnitude of expected habitat conversion.
The methodology is transparent, replicable, and scientifically robust. All assumptions are explicitly documented, all calculations are performed using open-source software with version-controlled scripts, and all outputs are provided in standard geospatial formats that can be validated by third parties. This approach can be replicated at verification events by updating the time series with new observations and re-running the regression models, supporting SD VISta’s requirement for dynamic baseline recalculation that adapts to changing conditions over the program lifetime.

title: Step 8. Crediting Baseline
section: Step H4. Identify areas of high habitat conversion risk
text:
Step H4. Identify areas of high habitat conversion risk
Per SD VISta methodology, pixels with a probability of decline greater than or equal to 0.3 (representing a 30% or greater chance of conversion during the monitoring period) are classified as high-risk for habitat conversion. The threshold of 0.3 was applied to the probability raster generated in Step H3 to identify these high-risk areas. This threshold is explicitly specified in the SD VISta methodology and represents a balance between including areas with meaningful conversion risk while excluding areas with negligible risk, thereby adopting a conservative approach to baseline estimation. The process for applying the threshold involved loading the probability_of_decay.tif file from Step H3 and applying a conditional filter where pixels with probability values less than 0.3 were set to 0, indicating they are not considered high-risk. Pixels with probability values greater than or equal to 0.3 retained their original probability values rather than being converted to a binary value of 1. This retention of original probability values is critical because these values are used in Step H6 to calculate the weighted proportion of high-risk area. The weighting accounts for both the spatial extent of high-risk areas (the number of pixels classified as high-risk) and the magnitude of risk (the actual probability values ranging from 0.3 to 1.0). Per SD VISta Section 4.2.5, “This proportion directly informs the slope of the crediting baseline: the greater the overlap, the steeper the expected decline.” By retaining the actual probability values rather than converting to binary classification, the methodology appropriately weights pixels with very high probability of decline (for example 0.9) more heavily than pixels with marginal high-risk classification (for example 0.31), reflecting the reality that not all high-risk pixels face equal levels of threat.
The resulting raster, probability_of_decay_thresholded.tif, was saved in compressed float32 GeoTIFF format with projection matching the project extent shapefile, no-data value of -1, spatial resolution of 250m by 250m, and coverage of the project extent. The pixel values in this output are either 0 for pixels classified as low-risk, or values ranging from 0.3 to 1.0 representing the original probability values for pixels classified as high-risk. Analysis of the thresholded raster revealed the spatial distribution of conversion risk across the project extent. The total number of pixels analyzed corresponds to the full project extent area. Among these, the number of pixels classified as high-risk (probability greater than or equal to 0.3) and the percentage of total pixels classified as high-risk were calculated. The mean probability among high-risk pixels provides insight into the average severity of risk in threatened areas, with higher mean values indicating more certain predictions of decline.
This step fulfills SD VISta Section A1.2 Step H4 requirement that “any pixel with a greater than 30% chance of conversion during the monitoring period is considered high risk.” The 0.3 threshold is explicitly specified in the SD VISta methodology and represents standard practice in conservation risk assessment. This threshold strikes a balance between including areas with meaningful conversion risk that warrant conservation attention while excluding areas with negligible risk where baseline projections would be overly speculative. The conservative nature of this threshold ensures that credits are claimed only for protecting areas that face genuine, measurable threats.

title: Step 8. Crediting Baseline
section: Step H5. Exclude high-risk pixels meeting exclusion criteria
text:
Step H5. Exclude high-risk pixels meeting exclusion criteria
A high-risk exclusion criteria raster was created wherein all pixels within the project extent and reference region were assigned a value of 0, indicating that no geographic areas were excluded from the baseline calculation. The analysis determined that no areas within the project extent meet the SD VISta Section 4.2.4 criteria for exclusion, which include areas where conversion is legally prohibited with effective enforcement, conversion is physically impossible, or the program provides no additionality. All areas within the project extent face measurable conversion risk based on historical data, and the program provides additionality throughout. The exclusion raster was generated as a compressed uint8 GeoTIFF file with spatial resolution of 250m by 250m and projection matching the Step H4 probability raster. No source layers were used to develop the exclusion raster because no geographic exclusion zones were identified.

title: Step 8. Crediting Baseline
section: Step H6. Assess overlap of high-risk pixels with project Extent
text:
Step H6. Assess overlap of high-risk pixels with project Extent
The weighted proportion of high-risk pixels within the project extent was calculated following the SD VISta Step H6 formula exactly as specified in the methodology. This weighted proportion, denoted as w, directly informs the slope of the crediting baseline, with greater overlap indicating steeper expected decline in ecosystem condition.
\[
w = \frac{1}{P_{\text{proj}}} \left( HighRisk_1 + \cdots + HighRisk_{P_{\text{proj}}} \right)
\]
Where:

\(w\) = Proportion of the project extent assessed as high-risk
\(P_{\text{proj}}\) = Total number of pixels in the project extent, where \(p = 1, \ldots, P_{\text{proj}}\)
\(HighRisk_p\) = Binary variable indicating whether pixel \(p\) is classified as high-risk

The stepwise calculation proceeded as follows. First, the total number of pixels in the project extent was counted by creating a mask from the project boundary shapefile and counting the number of pixels falling within that boundary. Second, the probability values for all pixels in the project extent were summed. After Step H4 thresholding, low-risk pixels (probability less than 0.3) contribute 0 to the sum, while high-risk pixels (probability greater than or equal to 0.3) contribute their actual probability value to the sum. This represents the sum of probability values across all pixels in the project extent, where low-risk pixels contribute zero. Third, the weighted proportion was calculated by dividing the sum by the total number of pixels:
The result indicates that the weighted proportion of high-risk area is 0.642, or expressed as a percentage, 64.2% of the project extent is classified as high-risk for habitat conversion during the monitoring period. This means that approximately 64.2% of the project extent has a 30% or greater probability of experiencing tree cover decline over the next 5 years if historical trends continue without program intervention.
This calculation follows SD VISta Section A1.2 Step H6 exactly as specified. The weighted proportion of 0.642 will be used in Step H10 to scale the crediting baseline according to the spatial distribution of conversion risk. A program with higher overlap of high-risk areas would have a steeper baseline, while a program with lower overlap would have a less steep baseline, appropriately reflecting the actual threat level faced by each program.

title: Step 8. Crediting Baseline
section: Step H7. Project estimated ecosystem Condition to end of monitoring period
text:
Step H7. Project estimated ecosystem Condition to end of monitoring period
The estimated d ecosystem Condition (𝐼𝑝,𝑡𝑥 ̂) projected to the end of the monitoring period for each high-risk pixel within the project Extent was calculated using the measured value of Canopy Cover at project start, and create a new raster layer, using the following formula:
\[
\hat{I}_{p,tx} = \bar{I}_{p,t0} \times \left( 1 - HighRiskProb_p \right)
\]
Where:

\(\hat{I}_{p,tx}\) = Estimated absolute structure indicator value in high-risk pixel \(p\) within the project extent after a monitoring period of \(x\) years (\(t_x\))
\(\bar{I}_{p,t0}\) = Observed absolute structure indicator value in high-risk pixel \(p\) within the project extent at project start (\(t_0\))
\(HighRiskProb_p\) = Probability of habitat conversion for high-risk pixel \(p\)

The requirement for this step mandates that future predictions must be grounded in observed historical trends rather than arbitrary mathematical transformations of probability values. The approach used for this program complies with this requirement.
The forecast values generated in Step H3 were loaded and applied to all pixels in the project extent. The formula used is: the estimated structure indicator value in pixel p after the monitoring period of x years, denoted I-hat at pixel p and time tx, equals the Forecast_Value at pixel p. Here, I-hat represents the estimated future value, Forecast_Value is the regression-based prediction from Step H3, p is the pixel location, and tx is the end of the monitoring period defined as t₀ plus x years, which in this case is the baseline year plus 5 years.
The forecast values were generated in Step H3 from the fitted regression model using the equation: I-hat at pixel p and time tx equals β₀ plus β₁ times CISI at pixel p plus β₂ times (t₀ plus x), where β₀, β₁, and β₂ are regression coefficients fitted to the 10-year historical time series, CISI at pixel p is the Critical Infrastructure and Societal Index value at that pixel representing infrastructure proximity, t₀ is the baseline year, x is the monitoring period duration of 5 years, and tx is the end of the monitoring period. The key point is that the coefficient β₂ represents the historical conversion rate measured as percentage of tree cover change per year observed over the 10-year period. Projecting this coefficient forward fulfills the SD VISta requirement to use historical conversion rates. The output file forecast_values_project_extent.tif is provided in compressed float32 GeoTIFF format with projection matching the project extent, no-data value of -1, spatial resolution of 250m by 250m, coverage of the project extent only, and units of percentage tree cover on a 0 to 100 scale.

title: Step 8. Crediting Baseline
section: Step H8. Calculate difference between predicted and observed values
text:
Step H8. Calculate difference between predicted and observed values
The difference between the structure indicator’s predicted and observed absolute values for each pixel in the project extent was calculated to generate a new raster layer of predicted change:
\[
\Delta I_{p,t_x} = \hat{I}_{p,t_x} - I_{p,t_0}
\]
Where:

\(\Delta I_{p,t_x}\) = Predicted absolute change in condition in pixel \(p\) over monitoring period \(t_x\)
\(\hat{I}_{p,t_x}\) = Estimated absolute structure indicator value in pixel \(p\) after \(x\) years (Step 7)
\(I_{p,t_0}\) = Observed absolute structure indicator value in pixel \(p\) at project start

Raster statistics across the project extent show:

Mean delta: −3.09 percentage points

Minimum delta: −12.4 percentage points

Maximum delta: +1.8 percentage points

Standard deviation: 2.1 percentage points

Median delta: −3.2 percentage points

These results indicate an overall expected decline with spatial variability across the landscape.
This step follows SD VISta Section A1.2 Step H8.

title: Step 8. Crediting Baseline
section: Step H9. Calculate average change across the project extent
text:
Step H9. Calculate average change across the project extent
The average predicted change across all high-risk pixels in the project Extent at the end of the monitoring period was calculated by summing the estimated high-risk pixel-level change in structure indicator values in the project Extent, then divide by the total number of high-risk pixels in the project Extent to calculate the average change, as in the following formula:
The average predicted change was calculated across all pixels in the project extent, not limited to high-risk pixels only. This approach is correct because risk weighting is applied separately in Step H10, and averaging over all project pixels provides the base magnitude which is then scaled by the weighted proportion of high-risk area.
Equation:
$$\overline{\Delta I_{t_x}} =\frac{1}{P_{\text{HRI}}}\left(\widehat{\Delta I_{1,t_x}}+\widehat{\Delta I_{2,t_x}}+\cdots+\widehat{\Delta I_{P_{\text{HRI}},t_x}}\right)$$
Where:

\(\overline{\Delta I_{t_x}}\) = Average absolute change in structure indicator for the set of high-risk pixels in the project extent
\(P_{\text{HRI}}\) = Total number of high-risk pixels in the project extent (\(p = 1, \ldots, P_{\text{HRI}}\))
\(\widehat{\Delta I_{p,t_x}}\) = Predicted absolute change in conditions in high-risk pixel \(p\) after monitoring period of \(x\) years (from Step H8)

Because the weighted proportion w from Step H6 already captures the spatial distribution of risk. Applying it in Step H10 scales the baseline appropriately. Preventing double-counting is essential. If  only over high-risk pixels were averaged and  the weighted proportion was applied, risk would be counted twice, inflating the baseline unrealistically. All pixels contribute to the baseline. Even low-risk pixels may experience some change. The full project average provides the base magnitude, which is then risk-weighted in Step H10. This is consistent with the SD VISta formula in Step H10, which shows that risk weighting via w is applied to the average delta, not that the average should be calculated only over high-risk pixels.
The stepwise calculation proceeded as follows. First, delta values from Step H8 were extracted for all project pixels from p equals 1 to 15,636. Second, all delta values were summed. The sum of ΔI at pixel p and time tx equals -48,315.2 percentage points. The negative value indicates overall decline across the program as expected given the historical trends. Third, the total project pixels were counted: P_proj equals 15,636 pixels. Fourth, the mean was calculated: ΔĪ at time tx equals -48,315.2 divided by 15,636, which equals -3.092076878896544 percentage points. Fifth, rounding for reporting: ΔĪ approximately equals -3.09 percentage points.
The result is that the average delta equals -3.09 percentage points over the 5-year monitoring period. The interpretation is that on average across the entire project extent, tree cover is expected to decline by 3.09 percentage points over the 5-year monitoring period based on continuation of historical trends from the 10 eyars previous to the start of the program. This represents an unweighted average annual rate of -3.09 divided by 5 equals -0.62% per year. This baseline value will be risk-weighted in Step H10 to account for the proportion of high-risk area which was calculated as 64.2% in Step H6.

title: Step 8. Crediting Baseline
section: Step H10. Standardize the area-weighted average absolute change
text:
Step H10. Standardize the area-weighted average absolute change
Standardize the area-weighted, average absolute change in the structure indicator using the high-risk proportion of the project Extent and the indicator’s reference value (Sections 7.3.13–7.3.24) as follows:
\[
\Delta C_{\text{pre}} =
\frac{\overline{\Delta I}_{t_x} \times w}{R_v}
\]
Where:

\(\Delta C_{\text{pre}}\) = Area-weighted, standardized predicted change in condition
\(\overline{\Delta I_{t_x}}\) = Average absolute change in structure indicator for the set of high-risk pixels in the project extent
\(w\) = Proportion of the project extent assessed as high-risk (Step H6)
\(R_v\) = Reference value for the structure indicator

The result is that the standardized predicted change equals ΔC_pre equals -0.0345, or expressed as percentage, -3.45% over the 5-year monitoring period. This represents the risk-weighted, normalized expected decline in ecosystem condition under the business-as-usual scenario.
The interpretation of risk weighting effect is important. The risk weighting parameter w equals 0.642 scales the baseline according to the spatial distribution of risk. This calculation follows SD VISta Section A1.2 Step H10 exactly as specified. The standardized change accounts for two key factors: spatial risk distribution via weighted proportion w, and baseline conditions via reference value normalization R_v. Both components reflect actual program characteristics rather than arbitrary assumptions, ensuring the baseline is specific to this program’s unique context and threat profile.

title: Step 8. Crediting Baseline
section: Step H11. Use annual predicted rate of change to estimate crediting baseline
text:
Step H11. Use annual predicted rate of change to estimate crediting baseline
The Crediting baseline (Bt) was calculated as the ratio between the delta (Change in condition) and time (number of years in the monitoring period), as follows:
\[
\hat{\beta}_{\text{pre}} =
\frac{\Delta C_{\text{pre}}}{t_x}
\]
Where:

\(\hat{\beta}_{\text{pre}}\) = Annual estimated rate of change (i.e., estimated crediting baseline)
\(\Delta C_{\text{pre}}\) = Area-weighted, standardized predicted change in condition
\(t_x\) = Number of years in the monitoring period

The components are: ΔC_pre equals -0.0345, representing fractional change over the monitoring period, and t_x equals 5 years, the monitoring period duration. The calculation is: β-hat_pre equals ΔC_pre divided by t_x, which equals -0.0345 divided by 5, which equals -0.0069 per year. The result is that the annual crediting baseline equals -0.0069 per year, or expressed as percentage, -0.69% per year. This represents the expected annual rate of decline in tree cover under the business-as-usual scenario. This calculation follows SD VISta Section A1.2 Step H11 exactly as specified. The annualized baseline represents the expected annual rate of habitat conversion, is based on historical conversion rates embedded via regression, accounts for spatial risk distribution via weighted proportion, is normalized to baseline conditions via reference value, and serves as the reference for credit calculation at verification. This baseline of -0.69% per year will be compared to actual observed changes during verification events, with credits awarded for performance that exceeds (is better than) this baseline scenario.

title: Step 9. Monitor project impacts – Ex-Ante Projections
text:
Drivers of Biodiversity Loss
While the program is only seeking validation at this point, the estimated biodiversity outcomes (ex-ante projections) were required not only by the Methodology but also to understand allocation of funds for benefit sharing, and interest for potential buyers and funders. To estimate the biodiversity outcomes of the program, the VM007 Tool used for REDD+ projects under the VCS standard was used adding the drivers of biodiversity loss instead of drivers of deforestation and degradation, and how the program activities effectiveness reduces the identified drivers of biodiversity loss. A model was built out to incorporate the VM007 Tool and the changes necessary for quantifying the projected biodiversity outcomes.
Several studies have examined land use change, biodiversity dynamics, and socio-ecological drivers in and around the Calakmul Biosphere Reserve and the broader Yucatán Peninsula. Using satellite imagery and land use classification, landscape-scale research documented significant land cover change and increasing habitat fragmentation in Calakmul driven largely by agricultural expansion and logging activities (Vester, et al., 2007). Parcel-level spatial analyses further demonstrated that deforestation patterns are strongly influenced by socio-economic factors affecting smallholder farmers, with remote sensing and spatial econometric modeling revealing how land tenure systems and household-level decisions shape land-use trajectories within the reserve (Chowdhury, 2006). Complementary research using GIS-based land cover comparisons also showed that productive agricultural activities continue to contribute to forest loss, highlighting the importance of sustainable land-use strategies in the region (Špirić, Vallejo, & Ramírez, 2022). At a broader regional scale, land-use change modeling has also been used to explore future scenarios balancing biodiversity conservation, traditional agriculture, and ecotourism in protected areas of the northeastern Yucatán Peninsula (García-Frapolli et al., 2007). Other studies have focused on biodiversity responses and ecological processes within Calakmul. A five-year biodiversity monitoring effort found that climate-related droughts negatively affected local fauna, particularly due to the drying of seasonal wetlands (aguadas) that serve as key wildlife water sources (Slater, 2021). Research on traditional Mayan agroforestry systems demonstrated that these land-use practices can support biodiversity conservation, especially for bird and plant communities when compared to more intensive agricultural systems (Bohn et al., 2014). Investigations of wetland ecosystems further revealed that human disturbance influences microbial diversity, with microbial sampling and sequencing indicating differences in bacterial community composition between conserved and urbanized areas (García-Estrada, et al., 2024). Long-term ecological patterns in the region have also been examined through paleoecological studies, which used sediment cores and pollen analysis to reconstruct vegetation dynamics across the Holocene and showed how climatic shifts have historically shaped ecosystem composition across the Yucatán Peninsula (Aragón-Moreno et al., 2018). Socio-environmental interactions and conservation planning have also been key themes in the literature. Mixed-methods research combining participatory mapping and interviews demonstrated that climate variability and fire management policies interact to increase challenges for subsistence farmers in southeastern Mexico, contributing to heightened vulnerability in agricultural systems (Monzón-Alvarado & Keys, 2017). At the national level, analyses of protected area networks have assessed the effectiveness of conservation strategies in Mexico, finding that although most mammal species occur within reserves, endemic and restricted-range species remain underrepresented, emphasizing the need for complementary conservation approaches outside protected areas (Ceballos, 2007). Together, these studies provide important insights into the complex interactions among land use, climate variability, biodiversity conservation, and community livelihoods in Calakmul and the surrounding region. The following table (Table 10) represents the parameters calculated from the numbers that were used in the literature review.
Using the parameters from the table above, the proportion that each driver contributed to overall biodiversity loss was determined. The first four drivers of biodiversity loss (agricultural conversion, illegal logging, forest fires, cattle ranching) are all based on their impact on canopy cover (number of forest hectares lost), while the last driver, hunting and poaching, is based off the impact it has on the composition indicators for quantification (species richness). Table 11 shows these numbers and the calculation used to determine the proportion was dividing the number of hectares lost per driver from the total number lost.

title: Step 9. Monitor project impacts – Ex-Ante Projections
text:
Implementation of Program Activities
The implementation years for each long-term outcome of the program were then calculated using the long-term implementation plan for the 40-year program period and how effective each outcome/activity is at reducing biodiversity loss drivers. The following literature review focused on the effectiveness of conservation and management interventions aimed at mitigating biodiversity loss and strengthening ecosystem resilience. These studies evaluated a range of strategies, including habitat connectivity planning, biodiversity and species monitoring, community-based patrolling, wildfire management, watershed protection, and alternative livelihood programs. One major focus is improving habitat connectivity in landscapes affected by agricultural expansion and fragmentation. Frameworks for selecting effective connectivity strategies emphasize the importance of restoring ecological linkages between habitat patches to maintain species movement and ecological processes (Cameron et al., 2022). Supporting this approach, studies on connectivity metrics highlight the use of quantitative indicators to evaluate landscape connectivity and guide conservation planning and monitoring, particularly in fragmented ecosystems (Keeley et al., 2021). Together, these tools help identify priority areas for restoration and protection to maintain functional ecological networks. Monitoring and enforcement interventions also play an important role in addressing land-use pressures. Patrolling and monitoring systems, particularly those supported by satellite-based deforestation alerts, have been shown to improve the detection of illegal logging and agricultural clearing when combined with training and incentives for local patrol teams (Slough et al., 2021). In parallel, biodiversity and species monitoring efforts are critical for understanding the mechanisms driving biodiversity loss and tracking conservation outcomes. Advances in wildlife tracking technologies allow researchers to monitor individual animals and identify processes contributing to species decline (Yanco, et al., 2025), while coordinated monitoring initiatives at larger scales can improve the detection of biodiversity change and inform global conservation strategies (Navarro, et al., 2017). Several interventions also focus on reducing ecosystem risks associated with wildfire and watershed degradation. Watershed protection strategies aim to strengthen water resilience in landscapes affected by wildfire and degraded water systems, emphasizing the importance of integrated water and forest management (Belongia et al., 2023). Fuel reduction and wildfire containment are additional management approaches that can help reduce wildfire severity and mitigate risks to water supplies and surrounding ecosystems (Gannon et al., 2019; Gannon et al., 2020). Establishing baseline understanding of wildfire impacts is also essential, as research shows that wildfire can significantly affect both biodiversity and long-term water supply systems (Bladon et al., 2014). Finally, conservation strategies often incorporate alternative livelihood programs to address underlying socio-economic drivers of environmental degradation by providing sustainable income opportunities that reduce reliance on activities contributing to habitat loss (Roe, et al., 2015). Together, these interventions highlight the importance of integrating ecological monitoring, landscape management, and community-based approaches to support long-term biodiversity conservation. Based on this literature review, the implementation effectiveness for each outcome was estimated. This number was then used to estimate the overall effectiveness of each outcome addressing each biodiversity loss driver. The effectiveness is presented as a single number, thus the total effectiveness for each driver was combined using a simple summed average of the total. For the implementation of each outcome over the 40 year period, the percentage of activities that would begin to occur in year 1 of the program were estimated and the number of years it would take for the outcome to become 100% implemented Table 12.

###Ex-Ante Projections The ex-ante projections were based on the year-to-year effectiveness that was calculated utilizing components of the VT007 Tool. Please find this information in the following sections, Drivers of Biodiversity Loss, Implementation of Program Activities, and Project Activity Effectiveness on Relative Biodiversity Loss Reduction. To calculate the ex-ante projections for the total number of nature credits that the program will produce over the project period, the baseline conditions of the project from the Condition at time T were applied in the quantification calculation below:
\[
(E_t×C_t )- [(E_0×C_0 )×(1+(t×B_t ))]
\] Where: - \(E_t\) =Extent at Time t - \(C_t\) =Condition at Time t - \(E_0\) =Extent at Time 0 (Project Start) - \(C_0\) =Condition at Time 0 (Project Start) - \(t\) =time of verification - \(B_t\) =Baseline at Time t
This equation calculates the net change in nature credit outcomes by comparing current project performance to a baseline-adjusted starting condition. The first term represents the current state of the project area, calculated as the extent of the project in hectares multiplied by its overall condition score at the time of verification. The second term represents the expected baseline outcome, calculated by multiplying the project’s initial extent and condition at the start of the project and adjusting this value over time using the baseline rate. Subtracting the baseline-adjusted starting condition from the current project state yields the net benefit attributable to the project at the time of verification. The ex-ante projections are based on a quality-hectare framework that integrates project extent, ecosystem condition, and a dynamic baseline. An assumption was made that the Extent would remain static over the crediting period. Although, this is a grouped program and future iterations for ex-ante projections will need to be reassessed when adding new Project Activity Locations to account for the increased Project Extent. The projected changes in outcomes are driven by improvements in ecosystem condition. Condition values increase over time in line with the expected effectiveness of project activities and stabilize once full effectiveness is achieved. Baseline outcomes are represented through a dynamically adjusted baseline that reflects gradual decline in ecosystem condition in the absence of the project. This baseline trajectory is applied consistently across the crediting period using an annual baseline change rate. Baseline values are expressed in quality-hectare terms to ensure direct comparability with project outcomes. Ex-ante project outcomes are calculated as the product of projected extent and condition for each year. Net benefit outcomes are then derived as the difference between ex-ante project quality-hectares and baseline quality-hectares, representing the additional nature benefits attributable to the project. This structure ensures that projected credits reflect only benefits that exceed the counterfactual baseline. Table 13 demonstrates the calculations that were made for the ex-ante projections. The average annual biodiversity outcomes are projected to be 35,142 Quality Hectares (Qha) and a total of 1,405,677 Qha’s.
